title: The Best Car I've Ever Driven: McLaren W1

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Cars have changed. Everything is smoother now, aerodynamically efficient, kind of blobs. Whatever happened to the super inspiring crazy supercar designs that we used to hang up on the bedroom wall as a poster as a kid?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Well, we still have those. So I did a video a couple years ago on hybrids and I'm not a prophet or anything but like the end point of that video was talking about how electrification is here. Hybrid supercars are here. The Holy Trinity from back in 2013 was just the beginning.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now it's 2024 and we've got this. The W1. The successor to the F1 and the P1. And those are some big shoes to fill. All of the groundbreaking driving performance and technology and experience that comes with every one-car is here. And I get to be one of the first people outside McLaren to drive it. Today's gonna be a fun day.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: A lot of people say the greatest supercar of all time is the McLaren F1. Still the fastest naturally aspirated car ever made: 240 miles per hour. And then its successor is my favorite car of all time, the McLaren P1. Member of the Holy Trinity of the first three hybrid supercars. I was lucky enough to get to drive one. Unreal car. The type of car where most owners that have 20 or 30 other cars still say that that's their favorite one to drive.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now the W1 is next up. From the powertrain to the aerodynamics to the track performance, it is closer than ever to a literal Formula One car with a license plate on it and I cannot wait to experience it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Alright. Time to drive it. Now I just gotta get into this thing, which I think is right under... Yeah. This is gonna be a squeeze.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So a hypercar like this is built around being the most track capable machine possible. But on the street, it still has to be a car. Still a really fast car obviously.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now you might be wondering how it compares to the Rimac Nevera that I got to make a video with. That was the world's fastest production car: all-electric, all-wheel drive, instant torque. And well, the Rimac is still faster in a straight line. That still beats everything.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But that's not the whole story. It may seem like McLaren is chasing everyone else by doing the trendy thing because this is yet another brand new thousand-plus horsepower hybrid.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But this is still a McLaren and they still do a lot of things that others simply don't these days. The extreme lightweight, the rear-wheel drive, hydraulic steering, 3D printed aerodynamic double wishbone suspension. Like, it's such a lightweight purist driver's machine.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: On the outside, this car is a masterpiece. Not just because it looks really good, but because it's an aerodynamic masterpiece. From the very, very front of this car all the way to the back, it's designed basically entirely to move air as efficiently and quickly from the front to the back.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So there's this active element right at the very front here, this front splitter actually moves up and down, it opens up for DRS and closes when you don't need that.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And the air starts by moving over the hood really quickly. The air that goes in that front piece here comes out the radiator and goes over that hood piece and keeps it glued to the top of the car.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You move air down the side, it gets sucked down in here and the air then channels along the side of the car through this massive inlet. I mean, the door is basically a bunch of holes.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And this top piece right here kind of looks like a roof scoop because the P1 had a roof scoop up there, but it's not. It's actually just a piece to keep the air continually moving over the top of the car.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then you get to the back with this active rear wing which does a lot of moving as we now know, and this huge rear diffuser which completes the ground force effects.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The air leaves the back of the car here twice as fast as it entered the front. And then you got that exhaust and uh, it's not bad looking.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So then this is the key to the McLaren W1.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So the thing about these McLaren interiors is they've been improving them a lot over the years and I think the biggest knock on McLaren in the past decade has probably been both reliability and build quality.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This is easily their best looking and most interesting interior of all time. It's crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: First of all, these seats are built into the carbon fiber structure of the car.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then I love this, this knit material is so interesting, it looks really cool, kind of like those concept cars I've seen at CES.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So I'll pull this handle down and this is what it's like in the driver's seat.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So there's a floating display over here which, believe it or not, has wired Apple Carplay. Not that you're going to be doing a whole bunch with the tech in this car.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But then there's a display in the back here and this whole binnacle is basically just like the way it is in some other modern McLarens like the Artura. You have your suspension controls on the left which can go up or down, and then your powertrain controls on the right which can also go up or down.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then this whole thing moves with the steering wheel. So if you move the whole thing up or down, looks pretty good.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then this material is everywhere, so there's Bowers and Wilkins speakers through the whole cabin, but like this speaker for example here has the material over it, but it should be acoustically transparent.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I think it looks really good. These carbon fiber aero ducts for your air conditioning, all of that looks fantastic.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There's a little hole in the seat here, there's also a, uh, fire extinguisher.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then a nice spot for the key here which is where I'm going to put it. But you can also move that back to expose another cup holder. So two cup holders, count 'em, in this car. Very practical, or maybe not.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: If you pull this little button right here, it gives you access to this back space behind the driver and the passenger which is good enough for maybe one and a half duffel bags. I'll call that a duffel bag and a backpack.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then of course since there's no other rear visibility here, you have a camera to have that rear visibility. So if you turn it off, that mirror sees nothing, but you turn it on and that camera is very useful even though the whole bottom quarter of it is taken up by the rear wing.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: That's cool, I like seeing the rear wing. It moves a lot when you drive. But yeah, this is a nice place to sit. Look, this, this handle right here, your...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Holding carbon fiber but that's also where you open the door. That, that's really nice. And then you have the speaker and you've got these little LEDs in here which I think you can see, which at night I'm sure is much more dramatic. And then you're essentially sitting in a carbon fiber tub. But here's one more crazy element, since the seats are fixed in this car, there's a little latch right here which you can pull to move these pedals towards you or away from you, which is how you adjust your seating position. You don't move the seat, you move the pedals away from you and the steering wheel into place where you want it. Nice big carbon fiber paddle shifters, a little bit of glass on the roof, and the world's thinnest sun visors, I think I'm comfortable saying that. These are like barely compliant thin carbon fiber sun visors, but hey, they get the job done. Then in the roof, you start the engine.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Comfort mode to start. The first things you notice obviously are being lower to the ground. It's pretty awesome actually. It rides really well. So these are not the best or worst roads in the world, these are actually pretty tight Italian mountain roads that they have us out here testing the car on. I'm noticing the car absorbs potholes really well in comfort mode. Not the loudest thing in the world, and the accelerator pedal is not too twitchy. Visibility is also, I think, actually pretty great. So the windshield's big. It's got this huge window next to me, and it's a split window, which means when I put this down, only the bottom half opens. I can pay the tolls and and wave to people.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then behind me, well there's nothing actually open behind me, but I am relying on this rearview camera, which the bottom fourth of it is basically entirely blocked by the rear wing, which is hilarious. It's not the worst visibility in the world. It's like B+ visibility for what this car is. The car is definitely wide and this is a pretty tight road, so I'm feeling that every time I turn. But the other thing I feel every time I turn is the hydraulic steering. I don't even have to be going that fast to feel that McLaren continues to do, in my opinion, the best steering in any supercar with their hydraulic steering.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: If you open up this car and explode it into thousand little pieces and look at the suspension parts, the double wishbone suspension that is 3D printed. So right now, it is, it's soft. Comfort mode is actually comfortable. The battery in comfort mode is just doing the torque fill to give you a responsive gas pedal at these lower RPMs that you cruise around at. And it's incredibly effective. It is actually a pretty tiny battery, it's just 1.4 kilowatt hours, but it's a radial flux e-motor straight from Formula E, so it is super power dense and you can even go up to one to two miles on electric-only before the engine actually has to turn on.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I don't want to bore anyone with too many technical details here, but I do think that these numbers will help you appreciate how ridiculous this car actually is. On the left here is my benchmark for a really, really fast car, that's the newest Porsche 911 Turbo S, and this is the W1. So on the left, the 630 horsepower is from the engine alone in the Porsche, but it's a hybrid, as you may know, so that adds 80 horsepower from the electric motor.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The W1 has almost twice as much horsepower, 1275, and over double the torque. It also weighs about 450 pounds less than the Porsche. And just looking at top speeds, the W1 is 217 versus the Turbo S at 205. The 0 to 62, 0 to 124, 0 to 186. McLaren's W1 basically doubles the acceleration numbers for the Porsche. This is really fast. Now we switch it into Sport Mode.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Okay, everything tightens up immediately. The car is louder, the suspension is stiffer. The steering is still excellent. This is where the car starts to feel alive. You can feel that torque fill from the electric motor, just filling in those gaps before the turbos really spool up. It's a seamless transition.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: ...system, totaling about 700 peak horsepower. It's very powerful. The W1, on the other hand, has a brand new in-house twin-turbo flat-plane-crank 4-liter V8 that makes over 900 horsepower by itself.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then a ridiculously powerful and highly developed electrical system that adds 342 more horsepower. So the totals are crazy, and the torque numbers are also crazy, but that's not the craziest thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The craziest thing is this car is also a featherweight. So these are the weights of the cars down here, 1700 kilograms, which is a normal car weight for a hybrid. 1399, that's the dry weight that McLaren has quoted for us, but let's add fuels and call it 1450 kilograms or something.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: That's still under 3200 pounds, which is insane. So the power-to-weight ratio of these cars, I mean they're in completely different leagues.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: All that to say that the 0 to 186 mile-per-hour time for this very fast German Autobahn missile is about 22 seconds. It's really fast. The W1 will do that in 12.7.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But now you know these cars, I've talked about them before, they have multiple personalities. This binnacle right above the steering wheel, just like the Artura and the other new McLarens, changes my powertrain and changes my suspension.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So I'm going to go to sport for both. So now everything stiffens up a bit. It's a little bit louder, it's a little more aggressive with the gear shifts and more responsive from the accelerator pedal.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So the sounds of this car really start to come alive more in sport mode. As you heat this thing up, you can hear I think the most prominent sound besides the engine is the turbo flutter.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The blow-off sound. I've driven McLaren 750S, you can watch some other McLaren review videos on the Auto Focus channel, but you do get these long pulls.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The gears are not as short as like the 750S and you get these long pulls with a ton of torque with the build from the hybrid system all the way from the bottom to the top of second gear.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There's eight gears to this gearbox. No reverse gear by the way, that's just with the electric motors. But man, this is such an interesting car.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It is on one hand, it represents a lot of what's happening in the rest of the hypercar world with what a lot of people actually don't really like that much to be frank, like of course a ton of horsepower, 1200 horsepower, crazy numbers, ridiculously capable but also a bit less communicative, a bit less emotion than some older cars.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But at the same time they're also doing a lot of what people really like, especially what McLaren's done well in the past, keeping the lightweight philosophy.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This is a 3000 pound car, that's crazy. Crazy power-to-weight ratio, amazing hydraulic steering, still very communicative in lots of ways.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But yeah, it's both things at once. And you know, you'll never be able to fully tell...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: about what this car is capable of on the streets like this,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: which means we should find that out by taking it to a track. Let's go to the track.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: For the track experience, McLaren has reserved Mugello Circuit for the day.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And this track has everything: 15 turns, twisting S curves, 50 feet of elevation change, and a long one kilometer straightaway through the start/finish line.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The Tuscan Grand Prix was actually hosted here in 2020, so it is literally an F1 grade track, perfectly smooth, which is a great opportunity to finally put the W1 in race mode,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: which drops the suspension and fully extends the rear wing back by almost a foot. It moves so far back that it essentially becomes an extension of the rear diffuser,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: which, fun fact, is actually no longer street legal because it extends past the rear bumper of the car. That will not be a concern here though.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This was a chance to finally actually open up full throttle, 1200 horsepower, and see what this car is capable of. It's the dream experience, if you ask me.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Tons of aero, super brisk at these high speeds. About the track now.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Tons of grip, and wow, a lot of power. Front up to make the curve so big there, make the line straighter.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Just put your finger on it. See how you really feel it kick in, don't you?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So how was that? Well, what a car. Yeah, it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Fun fact, I got to do a few more laps than this, but the footage is actually unusable because the G-forces from the car literally pulled the glass inside the lens

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: to move me out of focus, which is insane. The car stayed absolutely locked to the ground thanks to aerodynamics and those Trofeo RS tires.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I could only really get it to feel a little bit loose on the biggest braking zone on the whole track, fully stomping on those carbon ceramic brakes from 190 miles an hour,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: the back end wiggled a little bit into turn one. That's about it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: By the way, that speed at the end of that straight, over 190 miles an hour. Fun fact, that's the same speed Lewis Hamilton hit on his way to a victory here

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: during the Tuscan Grand Prix in that Formula One car. That is absolutely insane, and this is a car that can go on the street.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's clearly nuts, seriously a once in a lifetime opportunity for me.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Alright, conclusion time. I wrote down a bunch of my notes actually on my phone during the course of the day. Spent about three hours total in this car all day today between the track and street.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: My highlights for this car are: One, aesthetics. Because I mean, come on, anhedral doors.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I think this interior looks amazing. I think the materials are really nice and the build quality on top of that. This is also a prototype car, technically not finished, but I want to give it credit for that.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And two, the drive, the character, the way it can split between...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: between like a road-trippable car with actually a little bit of storage in it to a track monster, an aero monster, a power monster, is sick. It's it's what you should expect for how much you'd pay for this car. We'll get to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It is still a hybrid, which means all of the concerns that come with a hybrid. Obviously, this car did not throw zero faults at me today. Uh, it also had some weird door latch stuff, but again, it is a prototype, so I'll give it the pass for now. But there are concerns for long-term stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But I think McLaren in general has gotten way better with build quality and the things we've thought about with McLaren in the past. So, I'm optimistic.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The car that this reminds me the most of is the McLaren P1, which I was lucky enough to drive. It sounds a lot like the P1. It's ridiculously powerful and almost lag-free like the P1.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like a lot of these other cars, especially McLarens are very powerful and very light, and they're not laggy, but the turbos take some time, and this deletes that lag. And you can feel it while you hear the engine spool up, you feel yourself accelerate from the electric motor. It's awesome.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There's 399 being built. 2 million plus dollars. We're probably never going to see it on the road, just like P1. So the best chance you and I have of seeing this car is in videos like this.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So I hope I was able to do it justice. Again, the three hours I spent behind the wheel were pretty sick. I think I have a new poster car for the bedroom now. Thanks for watching. Catch you guys in the next. Peace.

highlights: The McLaren W1 is the hybrid successor to the legendary F1 and P1, featuring a twin-turbo V8 and a Formula E-derived electric motor that produce a combined 1,275 horsepower.
To maintain a lightweight "purist" driving experience, the car remains under 3,200 pounds and utilizes hydraulic steering instead of the electric systems found in most modern supercars.
The vehicle is an aerodynamic masterpiece with an active rear wing that extends nearly a foot in "Race Mode," creating enough downforce to achieve track speeds comparable to a Formula One car.
The interior features an innovative design where the carbon fiber seats are fixed directly to the chassis, requiring the driver to move the pedals and steering wheel to adjust their seating position.
With a 0–186 mph time of just 12.7 seconds and a price tag exceeding $2 million, the W1 is a highly exclusive hypercar limited to only 399 production units.
summary:
In this transcript, Marques Brownlee reviews the McLaren W1, the highly anticipated successor to the legendary F1 and P1 hypercars. Positioned as a "Formula One car with a license plate," the W1 is a hybrid powerhouse delivering 1,275 horsepower and over 1,000 lb-ft of torque through a combination of a twin-turbo V8 and a Formula E-derived electric motor. Brownlee emphasizes the car’s "purist" engineering, noting that despite its complex hybrid system, McLaren maintained a lightweight profile (under 3,200 lbs) and retained signature elements like hydraulic steering for superior driver feedback.

The review highlights the W1’s extreme focus on aerodynamics and interior innovation. The exterior is described as an "aerodynamic masterpiece" featuring active front splitters and a massive rear wing that extends nearly a foot in race mode to act as an integrated diffuser. Inside, the car features seats integrated directly into the carbon fiber tub, requiring the driver to adjust the pedals and steering wheel rather than the seat itself. While the interior is McLaren's most refined to date, featuring unique knit materials and modern tech, the car remains a track-focused machine with minimal storage and limited rear visibility compensated for by a digital camera.

During track testing at the Mugello Circuit, Brownlee finds the W1’s performance staggering, matching Formula One speeds of 190 mph on the straightaways and exhibiting immense grip. He concludes that while the $2 million, limited-production vehicle faces the typical reliability questions associated with high-end hybrids and prototypes, it successfully bridges the gap between the raw emotion of the P1 and modern technological capability. Ultimately, he frames the W1 as a new "poster car" that represents the pinnacle of McLaren's engineering heritage.
description:
I may never see this car again. But it's incredible.

topics: The legacy and evolution of the McLaren '1' series from the F1 and P1 to the W1
Technical engineering, aerodynamic design, and lightweight construction
Performance benchmarking against the Porsche 911 Turbo S and Rimac Nevera
Interior ergonomics, materials, and cabin technology
Driving impressions on Italian public roads and the Mugello track
